name: Left Anterior Fascicular Block
aliases: Left Axis Deviation due to LAFB
identifyBy: QRS duration <120ms (narrow QRS - not a full BBB)
Small R, deep S in II, III, aVF (rS pattern inferior)
Small Q, tall R in I, aVL (qR pattern lateral)
No other cause of left axis deviation (e.g. not inferior MI, not WPW)
management: Investigate for cause: hypertension, aortic valve disease, ischaemic heart disease, cardiomyopathy
LAFB + RBBB = bifascicular block - monitor for progression to complete heart block
Trifascicular block (bifascicular + 1° AV block): cardiology referral - pacemaker consideration
pearls: LAFB is the most common fascicular block - present in 4% of ECGs
Left axis deviation has many causes: LAFB, inferior MI, WPW, hyperkalaemia, LV pacing
The left bundle has 2 fascicles: anterior (LAFB → left axis) and posterior (LPFB → right axis)
LAFB does not prolong QRS significantly - unlike full BBB
In the context of anterior MI, LAFB may indicate periinfarction block - worse prognosis
differentials: Inferior MI (left axis with Q waves in II, III, aVF)
WPW with negative delta in inferior leads

qaNotes: Conduction abnormality pattern: QRS/axis morphology should be checked in diagnostic leads.
